text:
The weekend is that cruel, fleeting mirage that appears on the horizon every Friday afternoon, only to vanish by Sunday evening with all the permanence of a soap bubble. You spend the entire work week in a state of anticipatory delirium, mentally checking off the days like a prisoner scratching marks on a cell wall, dreaming of all the things you'll finally accomplish when you're free. You'll reorganize the garage. You'll meal prep for the week. You'll read that book that's been sitting on your nightstand since the Clinton administration. You'll become a functional human being again.

Then Saturday morning arrives and you wake up at 6 AM out of pure habit, and the crushing realization sets in: you have exactly forty-eight hours to undo five days of accumulated chaos, existential dread, and the slow erosion of your will to live. By the time you've had your coffee and scrolled through your phone for what you thought was five minutes but was actually ninety, you've already lost two hours. The weekend is now officially in its "back half," which is a term that should only exist in physics textbooks, not in a person's leisure time.

You manage to accomplish exactly one of your weekend goals, usually the least important one. The garage remains unorganized. Your meal prep consists of ordering takeout. That book gets moved to a new shelf, which counts as progress. By Sunday evening, you're staring down the barrel of another work week with the same broken promises hanging over your head, plus the added bonus of Sunday scoop: that special time when your anxiety begins its nightly ascent and you remember that you have emails to answer.

The weekend needs a union representative. It needs to negotiate for better hours.
